speaker: patient
text: Hi, Doctor. I’ve been feeling really off lately—fatigued, cold all the time, and my vision’s been blurry.

speaker: doctor
text: Thanks for coming in. Let’s start with your labs from today: your blood glucose came back at 15.2 mmol/L—significantly elevated.

speaker: doctor
text: Based on your symptoms and lab work, your primary diagnosis is hypothyroidism—we’ll confirm with TSH and free T4 shortly.

speaker: patient
text: Hypothyroidism? That makes sense—I’ve had dry skin and constipation too. But what about the high blood sugar? Is that related?

speaker: doctor
text: Good question. We’ll investigate whether this is new-onset diabetes or secondary to thyroid dysfunction. Let’s review your other vitals and labs.

speaker: patient
text: I also noticed my vision got blurry just a few days ago—like things are hazy, especially when reading.

speaker: patient
text: Blurred vision has been persistent since Sunday—worse in the mornings.

speaker: doctor
text: Your hemoglobin today is 161 g/L—within normal range, so anemia isn’t contributing to fatigue.

speaker: patient
text: My blood pressure was 134 over 82 at home yesterday. Does that matter?

speaker: doctor
text: Yes—it’s relevant. Your systolic pressure today is 134 mmHg, which is mildly elevated but not urgent. We’ll monitor it closely given your glucose levels.

speaker: patient
text: I’ve been taking levothyroxine 50 mcg daily for two weeks, but I’m still tired. And my appetite hasn’t changed much.

speaker: doctor
text: We’ll adjust your levothyroxine dose after reviewing your TSH. In the meantime, let’s address the hyperglycemia—have you checked your glucose at home?

speaker: patient
text: Yes—I’ve been testing fasting and post-meal. It’s been between 14 and 16 most days.

speaker: doctor
text: That aligns with our concern. Let’s repeat hemoglobin—we’ll check it again to assess for any acute change.

speaker: doctor
text: The repeat hemoglobin is now 137 g/L—down from 161 earlier today. That suggests possible sample variation or early hemodilution; we’ll recheck tomorrow.

speaker: patient
text: Could the blurred vision be from the high sugar? My eyes feel strained.

speaker: doctor
text: Very likely—acute hyperglycemia can cause osmotic lens swelling and transient refractive changes. We’ll refer you for urgent ophthalmology if it persists beyond glucose control.

speaker: patient
text: I tested again just before coming in—my glucose was 16.0 mmol/L. I’m worried it’s getting worse.

speaker: doctor
text: That’s concerning—16.0 mmol/L confirms persistent hyperglycemia. I am going to start you on basal insulin temporarily while we investigate further.

speaker: patient
text: Actually, when I double-checked that first reading—the one you told me was 15.2—that number matches what I saw on my meter this morning too.
